1: The genealogy resumes the sons of Judah from a different entry point than ch2 — Perez, Hezron, Carmi, Hur, and Shobal. The formula here is bᵉnê Yᵉhûdāh (sons of Judah), the standard opening for a new genealogical unit. The five names bridge the main Judahite lines descended from ch2, grouping together diverse sub-clans under the Judahite heading.
2: The genealogical verb hôlîd (hiphil of yālad) means “caused to beget” or “fathered” — the standard causative form for genealogical chains. The MKT renders it “fathered” throughout, preserving the Hebrew's emphasis on the active role of the father in the chain rather than the more neutral English “was the father of.”
3: The formula ʾăbî (father of) appears before place names throughout this chapter — ʾăbî Ētām, “father of Etam.” In genealogical contexts, ʾāḇ before a place name connotes “founder” or “settler” of that location. The MKT renders this “founder of” to capture the sense that these individuals established settlements, not merely that they had children named after towns.
4: Pᵉnûʾēl ʾăbî Gᵉdōr wᵉʿEzer ʾăbî Ḥûšāh — two founder-formulas in parallel. Pᵉnûʾēl may carry the resonance of the Jabbok crossing (Gen 32:30, “face of God”), though here it is a personal name. Ḥûšāh likely corresponds to the town from which Hushai the Archite came (2 Sam 15:32). The closing attribution “sons of Hur, firstborn of Ephrathah, father of Bethlehem” identifies this line's connection to Bethlehem through Hur.
5: Ašḥûr ʾăbî Tᵉqôaʿ — founder of Tekoa. Tekoa was the home of Amos the prophet (Amos 1:1) and the wise woman summoned by Joab (2 Sam 14:2). The two wives, Ḥelāh and Naʿărāh, introduce a brief chiastic genealogy: Naarah's sons in v6, Helah's in v7.
6: The sons of Naarah are listed first, then Helah's (v7) — the order reverses normal birth-order priority, perhaps reflecting a significance judgment by the Chronicler. The names are otherwise unattested and carry no clear etymology relevant to the commentary; the list preserves clan identity records that the post-exilic community needed for land and inheritance disputes.
7: The sons of Helah close the Ashhur sub-unit. Yiṣhar (from yāṣhar, “to shine/be bright”) appears also as a Levitical name in Exod 6:18. The Chronicler includes both branches of Ashhur's family, giving equal genealogical standing to both wives' descendants.
8: Qôṣ hôlîd ʾet-ʿAnûb — Koz fathered Anub. Qôṣ (thorn) is a name that may reflect the topography of the clan's territory. Zobebah (שֹׁובֵבָה, from šûḇ, “to return”) appears to be a personal or clan name unique to this verse. The formula mišpᵉḥôt ʾAḥarḥēl (clans of Aharhel) indicates the Chronicler is working from clan-registry sources in addition to personal genealogies.
9: Yaʿbēṣ (Jabez) is explicitly glossed by the narrator: ʾāmᵉrāh ʾimmô qārāʾtî šᵉmô yaʿbēṣ lēʾmōr kî yāladtî bᵉʿōṣeḇ — “his mother named him Jabez, saying, 'I bore him in pain/sorrow.'” The name yaʿbēṣ inverts the letters of ʿōṣeḇ (sorrow/pain) in a folk etymology. The Chronicler introduces Jabez mid-genealogy as “more honoured than his brothers” (niḵbad miʾaḥāyw) — a signal to the reader that an exceptional figure follows.
10: The prayer of Jabez is dense with literary technique. ʾim-bārēḵ tᵉḇārᵉḵēnî — the infinitive absolute (bārēḵ) before the finite verb (tᵉḇārᵉḵēnî) intensifies the plea: “if you would indeed bless me.” wᵉhirbîtā ʾet-gᵉḇûlî — “enlarge my territory,” using the hiphil of rāḇāh (to be many/great). The prayer closes with lᵉḇiltî ʿoṣbî — “that I might not have pain,” returning to the ʿōṣeḇ root of his name. The prayer is thus a lifelong response to the sorrow of his birth: he asks God to write a different story than his name predicted.
11: Kᵉlûḇ ʾăḥî Šûḥāh hôlîd ʾet-Mᵉḥîr — the formula is straightforward. Kᵉlûḇ is a variant spelling of Kālēḇ (Caleb), which may or may not be the same figure as the spy of Numbers 13. The genealogical chain here uses the standard hôlîd causative, tracking through Mechir to Eshton in v12.
12: Ešṭōn hôlîd ʾet-Bêt-rāpāʾ — Eshton fathered Beth-rapha (“house of healing/giant”). Ir-Naḥāš (city of serpent/bronze) is otherwise unknown geographically but likely a Judahite settlement. ʾanšê Rēkāh (men of Recah) indicates the Chronicler is again working from guild or clan-settlement records whose names have not survived elsewhere.
13: bᵉnê Qᵉnaz — Kenaz was an Edomite clan chief (Gen 36:11) who became assimilated into Judah, so Othniel the Kenizzite (Judg 1:13; 3:9-11), Israel's first judge, descended from a non-Israelite ancestor. The name Othniel is possibly from ʿōz (strength) + ʾēl (God). The reference here is brief; the fuller picture comes from Judges.
14: Gêʾ-Ḥărāšîm — “Valley of Craftsmen,” from ḥārāš (craftsman, artisan, metalworker). The phrase kî ḥărāšîm hāyû (“for they were craftsmen”) explains the place name — a settlement of artisans in the service of the Judahite kingdom, perhaps supplying military equipment. This is one of the Chronicler's incidental socioeconomic footnotes preserved nowhere else.
15: bᵉnê Kālēḇ ben-Yᵉphunneh — Caleb son of Jephunneh, the spy who gave a positive report (Num 13:30; 14:6-9). This Caleb is distinct from the Calebites of v11-12 who may or may not be the same genealogical line. The name Elah (אֵלָה, terebinth tree) is a common theophoric/nature name in the HB.
16: The sons of Jehallelel: four names, none of whom appear elsewhere. The genealogical function is territorial — recording which sub-clans held Judahite land in the Chronicler's administrative framework. The formula bᵉnê X without the hôlîd chain indicates a horizontal listing of clan members rather than a vertical father-son chain.
17: The genealogy of Ezrah introduces Miriam — a female name notable in this context. The Chronicler occasionally inserts women's names into otherwise male genealogies when they were clan founders or significant mothers (cf. Shelomith in 2 Chr 11:20). Mered (rebellion) appears to have taken two wives: an Egyptian wife (v18) and a Jewish wife (v18b), giving him two distinct lines of descendants.
18: ʾiššātô hayᵉhûdîyyāh — “his Jewish wife” — the explicit ethnic marker “Judahite/Jewish” (yᵉhûdîyyāh) is needed because Mered had an Egyptian wife (named Bithiah/Bityah in v18, “daughter of Yah”). The name Bithiah is possibly a Hebraized form honoring a pious Egyptian woman, or it may reflect an Israelite name given at conversion — the daughter of Pharaoh identified in Exod 2:5-10 is sometimes connected with this Bithiah in rabbinic tradition.
19: The sons of Hodiah's wife, “sister of Naham” — Hodiah married a woman whose clan identity comes through her brother, not her father. Kᵉîlāh haGarmî (Keilah the Garmite settlement) — Garmî is of uncertain origin; possibly connected to gerem (bone/substance) as a place name. Eštemoa hamaʿăḵātî — Eshtemoa the Maachathite — the Maachathites were a people east of the Jordan (Deut 3:14), so this entry records mixed-ancestry clans within Judah.
20: Sons of Shimon and Ishi: a brief four-name and two-name list. Ben-Ḥānān (son of gracious one) and Ben-Zōḥēt (son of Zoheth) use ben (son of) as part of the actual name — these are not genealogical formulas but hypocoristic names that incorporate ben as a prefix, similar to Benjamin.
21: bᵉnê Šēlāh ben-Yᵉhûdāh — Shelah was Judah's third son (Gen 38:5), whose line was passed over at Tamar's double deception to allow Perez and Zerah to take precedence. The inclusion here rehabilitates Shelah's descendants as legitimate Judahites. Bêt-ʿăḇodāt-bûṣ — “house of linen workers” or “house of the guild of linen workers.” The ʿăḇôdāh (service/work) term applied to a textile guild suggests organized craft production, perhaps in royal service.
22: This verse contains a textual difficulty: wᵉyāšuḇî Lehem — the MT reads “and they returned to Lehem (Bethlehem).” The LXX reads differently; some scholars emend to a place name, others retain a verb meaning “returned.” The note “the records are ancient” (wᵉhaddᵉḇārîm ʿattîqîm) is the Chronicler's own acknowledgment that his sources for this material are antiquarian — he is working from old clan registers, not living memory.
23: hāyôṣᵉrîm — “the potters” (from yāṣar, to form/shape, the same root used for YHWH forming man in Gen 2:7). The phrase yōšᵉḇê-šām ʿim-hammelek bimlāʾktô — “they dwelt there in the king's service” — indicates royal workshops. The Chronicler preserves evidence of royal craft guilds operating under patronage from a Judahite king, a practice well-attested in ANE administrative texts.
24: bᵉnê Šimʿôn — the shift to Simeon's genealogy is significant. Simeon received no separate tribal territory in Canaan but was absorbed within Judah's allotment (Josh 19:1-9). The Chronicler's inclusion of Simeon validates their continued existence as a tribe even within Judah's geographic territory. The five names here differ slightly from Gen 46:10 and Num 26:12-14 — normal variation in genealogical transmission.
25: The tôlᵉdôt chain for Simeon (Shallum, Mibsam, Mishma) uses the standard linear formula: bᵉnô X bᵉnô Y — “his son X, his son Y” — indicating a direct father-to-son chain rather than the sibling-list of v24. Mibsam also appears as an Ishmaelite name (Gen 25:13), suggesting a possible tribal overlap or shared naming tradition.
26: The chain continues through Mishma's descendants. bᵉnê Mišmāʿ — sons of Mishma — then lists three consecutive bᵉnô links: Hamuel, Zaccur, Shimei. This is the deepest Simeonite genealogy preserved in the HB and likely drawn from clan records maintained through the Assyrian and Babylonian periods.
27: Šimʿî šiššāh-ʿāśār bānîm wᵉšēš bānôt — Shimei had sixteen sons and six daughters. The unusual female count (bānôt = daughters) is recorded here and again stands out in the genealogical framework. The theological note follows: “his brothers did not multiply greatly, and their whole clan did not equal the sons of Judah.” The comparison to Judah frames the Simeonite genealogy as an explanation for Simeon's absorption into Judah — smaller population means less territorial claim.
28: The settlement list for Simeon: Beer-sheba, Moladah, Hazar-shual — cities at the southern edge of Judah's territory, in the Negev. Beer-sheba was the southernmost city of the traditional settlement (“from Dan to Beer-sheba”). The phrase establishes the Simeonites as desert-edge settlers rather than highland farmers like Judah.
29: Bilhah, Ezem, Tolad — southern Negev settlements listed in Josh 15:29-30 and 19:3-4 as Judahite and Simeonite towns respectively. The Chronicler's list slightly diverges from Joshua in names and spelling, reflecting either textual transmission variation or different administrative periods of settlement.
30: Bethuel, Hormah, Ziklag — Hormah (ḥormāh, “devoted to destruction”) was where Israel was routed after the failed Kadesh incursion (Num 14:45; 21:3). Ziklag became David's base during his time with the Philistines (1 Sam 27:6; 30:1). The Chronicler's inclusion of Ziklag in the Simeonite list establishes the tribal claim that David later inherited by grant.
31: ʿad-mᵉlōk Dāwîd — “until the time of David” — a chronological marker establishing the antiquity of these settlement records. The Chronicler is saying that these were the Simeonite cities prior to the Davidic reorganization of tribal territories. The formula “these were their cities” closes the settlement list formally.
32: Five ḥăṣērôt (villages/enclosures) are listed. The Hebrew ḥāṣēr (pl. ḥăṣērôt) typically denotes unwalled settlements — hamlets or encampments — in contrast to fortified ʿārîm (cities). The Simeonite settlements were thus smaller, unwalled communities in the semi-arid southern zone.
33: ûmišpaḥōtām (and their genealogical records) — the formal close of the Simeonite list with the phrase “they maintained their genealogical records” (yiḥaśśᵉmû lāhem). This is the Chronicler's notation that these clan lists come from official genealogical registers (yaḥas, genealogy/registration), not oral tradition.
34: A new list begins with Meshobab and eleven additional Simeonite leaders. The transition from settlement list to leader list reflects different source materials: vv28-33 came from a geographic-administrative record; vv34-43 come from a clan-leadership register. The leaders' names are given with patronymics to several generations depth, indicating their importance.
35: wᵉYôʾēl wᵉYēhûʾ ben-Yôšiḇyāh ben-Śᵉrāyāh ben-ʿĂśîʾēl — a four-generation patronymic for Jehu, establishing his distinguished ancestry within the Simeonite leadership. The depth of genealogy (four generations) signals significant status; ordinary clan members warranted only one or two generations of identification.
36: Seven more names in the Simeonite leadership roster. ʾĔlyôʿênay (my eyes are toward YHWH) is a theophoric name reflecting post-exilic piety; its presence in this list suggests the register was updated or supplemented during the restoration period when such names became common.
37: Zîzāʾ ben-Šipî ben-ʾAllôn ben-Yᵉdāyāh ben-Šimrî ben-Šᵉmaʿyāh — six-generation patronymic for Ziza, the deepest in this section. Six generations of tracing suggests either an exceptionally prominent ancestor or a clan that took great pride in establishing its genealogical credentials in the post-exilic period.
38: habbāʾîm bᵉšēmôt — “those listed by name” — a formal designation for officially registered clan heads. The phrase wᵉnāśîʾîm bᵉmišpᵉḥōtām (leaders in their clans) establishes their administrative role. wᵉḇêt ʾăḇōtêhem pāraṣ lārōḇ — their families multiplied greatly — the expansion of Simeon in the following verses is presented as blessing (the verb pāraṣ, “to burst forth/spread,” is used of rapid growth, as in Gen 28:14 for Jacob's descendants).
39: wayyēlᵉkû lᵉmôḇôʾ Gᵉdōr — “they journeyed to the entrance of Gedor.” The location is uncertain — possibly Gerar (reading with LXX) in Philistine territory. lirʿôt migrᵉhem — “to find pasture for their flocks” — the motive is pastoral expansion, not military aggression. The Simeonite movement is framed as a search for grazing land, not conquest.
40: wayyimṣᵉʾû mirʿeh šāmēn wāṭôḇ — “they found rich, good pasture” — the language echoes the description of the Promised Land (Deut 8:7-9). šeqeṭet wĕšalᵉwāh — “peaceful and quiet” — the two adjectives together describe ideal pastoral conditions. miḥām (Ham) as the former inhabitants signals Canaanite origin; the displacement of Ham's descendants for Israel's tribes echoes the original conquest narrative.
41: hakkᵉtûḇîm bᵉšēmôt — “listed by name” (same formula as v38) — specifying these are the officially registered Simeonite leaders. The campaign is dated to Hezekiah king of Judah, providing a synchronization point with the Judean royal chronology. wayyakkûm (they struck them down) and wayyaḥᵉrîmûm (they devoted them to destruction) — the ḥērem verb (ḥāram in hiphil) applies the holy-war ban to the surviving Canaanite enclaves in the Negev.
42: ḥămiššāh meʾôt ʾîš — five hundred men, a smaller raiding party for the Mount Seir campaign. The four leaders (Pelatiah, Neariah, Rephaiah, Uzziel) are named; they are sons of Ishi (cf. v20). Mount Seir was Edomite territory (Gen 32:3; 36:8), suggesting Simeonite expansion eastward as well as southward, filling the vacuum left by Assyrian disruption of established populations.
43: wayyakkû ʾet-šᵉʾērît happālēṭāh laʿămālēq — “they struck down the remaining Amalekites who had escaped.” Saul had failed to complete the destruction of Amalek (1 Sam 15); David had driven them back (1 Sam 27:8; 30); now Simeonite clan warriors complete what royal campaigns had not. wayyēšᵉḇû šām ʿad hayyôm hazzeh — “they have lived there to this day” — the narrator's present-time anchor for the Simeonite settlement in Edomite territory.
